summary: Patrick McKenzie provides a comprehensive insider's guide to doing business in Japan, drawing on his decade of experience as a foreign entrepreneur. The essay centers on the salaryman system as the defining institution of Japanese professional life, explaining how it creates a near-total dependency between employee and employer that pervades housing, relationships, social life, and identity. This system makes startups extraordinarily risky in social terms, constrains hiring, and shapes how software is sold (high-touch, relationship-driven). Despite these challenges, McKenzie argues Japan represents a massively underserved market with weak competition, where even adequate technology feels like wizardry from the future. He balances frank discussion of systemic racism and bureaucratic friction with genuine affection for Japan's culture of personal service and relationship-based trust.
key_points: The salaryman system functions as a total institution: employees trade absolute loyalty for lifetime security, and opting out permanently damages one's social standing and career prospects
Japanese startups must hire from the margins of society — women, foreigners, and 'misfits' — because the salaryman system monopolizes conventional talent
Selling software in Japan virtually requires high-touch, relationship-driven sales even for low price-point products, making enterprise software more viable than SMB software
Japan's technology market is 5-10 years behind the US skill curve in web/mobile but represents a massive, underserved opportunity with weak competition from both startups and megacorps
Being a foreigner in Japan involves navigating real institutional discrimination alongside a culture of extraordinary personal service and loyalty
money_quotes: The salaryman/employer relationship is best characterized as 'You swear yourself to us, body and soul, and in return we will isolate you from all risks.'
Most people want to become wealthy so they can consume social status. Japanese employers believe this is inefficient, and simply award social status directly.
Otaku, Koreans, foreigners, dropouts, I'll hire anybody who can do the work. You're bargains.

In an ideal world there would be no racists, but in the less than ideal world that you may find yourself living in, at least hope to run into ruthlessly capitalist racists, because that's something you can work with.
UX and design which Silicon Valley companies would consider barely adequate for an internal admin app would strike Japanese customers like wizardry from the future.
I was immediately asked to leave the apartment 'at your first available convenience' because 'self-employed' is about one half-step above 'homeless vagabond' in terms of social esteem in Japan.
There exists a customer of the bank who habitually makes USD wire transfers using Lloyds and, accordingly, it is my business to know this.
Few things in life are worth fighting over. Fights that are worth fighting are usually worth winning.
tone: candid, witty, deeply informed, affectionate yet unflinching
key_insight: Japan's salaryman system creates a total social institution so powerful that opting out carries enormous personal risk, but this same rigidity leaves the market massively underserved by technology, creating extraordinary opportunities for those willing to navigate the cultural complexity.
